meta.title: SF-SPAP-1: Platform Fundamentals, Loading and Querying

questions.stem: Which Snowflake layer stores table data in compressed columnar files?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the Storage layer persists all table data as compressed, columnar micro-partition files in cloud object storage.

**A** is incorrect because Cloud Services handles metadata management, query optimization, and authentication — it does not store table data.

**C** is incorrect because virtual warehouses provide compute resources for query execution; they read from Storage but do not persist data themselves.

**D** is incorrect because Marketplace is a discovery and distribution channel for data products, not a storage layer.

[Snowflake Architecture Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts)

questions.stem: What is a virtual warehouse used for in Snowflake?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because virtual warehouses are clusters of compute resources that execute SQL queries, DML statements, and data loading operations.

**A** is incorrect because micro-partitions are stored in the Storage layer, not inside a warehouse.

**C** is incorrect because role hierarchy is a security concept managed through Cloud Services and RBAC, not by warehouses.

**D** is incorrect because publishing listings is a Marketplace/Sharing capability, unrelated to warehouse compute.

[Virtual Warehouses Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview)

questions.stem: Which statement about Snowflake compute and storage is correct?

options.text: Adding users requires a larger warehouse
Data sharing copies table data into each consumer account

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Snowflake's architecture decouples compute from storage, allowing each to scale independently based on workload and data volume needs.

**A** is incorrect because storage scales automatically as data grows; resizing warehouses affects compute capacity only.

**C** is incorrect because user count does not determine warehouse size; warehouse sizing is based on query concurrency and complexity.

**D** is incorrect because Snowflake's secure data sharing exposes live data by reference — no physical copy is made in the consumer account.

[Key Concepts & Architecture](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts)

questions.stem: Which object hierarchy is valid in Snowflake?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the Snowflake hierarchy flows from Organization (top level) to Account, then to Database, Schema, and finally Table (or other data objects).

**B** is incorrect because Organizations contain Accounts, not the other way around; warehouses are also not part of the data object hierarchy.

**C** is incorrect because Account is the root logical container under Organization; databases do not contain accounts or warehouses.

**D** is incorrect because Roles are security principals, not structural object containers in the hierarchy.

[Understanding Snowflake Database Objects](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/snowflake-db)

questions.stem: Which interface is specifically a command-line client for Snowflake?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because SnowSQL is Snowflake's dedicated command-line interface (CLI) for connecting to Snowflake and running SQL commands from a terminal.

**A** is incorrect because Snowsight is the browser-based web UI, not a CLI tool.

**C** is incorrect because Snowpipe is a continuous data ingestion service, not a client interface.

**D** is incorrect because Snowpark is a developer API framework for Python, Java, and Scala — not a CLI.

[SnowSQL (CLI Client)](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/snowsql)

questions.stem: A team needs 90 days of Time Travel for selected tables. Which edition requirement matters most?

options.text: Enterprise Edition or higher is required
Business Critical is the only option

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Time Travel retention beyond 1 day (up to 90 days) requires Enterprise Edition or higher.

**A** is incorrect because Standard Edition supports a maximum Time Travel retention of only 1 day.

**C** is incorrect because warehouse size has no effect on Time Travel retention period — it is governed by account edition and the `DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS` parameter.

**D** is incorrect because Business Critical is not the only qualifying edition; Enterprise Edition already supports the 90-day maximum.

[Understanding & Using Time Travel](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-time-travel)

questions.stem: Which activities are handled by the Cloud Services layer? (Select TWO)

options.text: Persisting table files in object storage
Running every query operator directly on raw data

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the Cloud Services layer performs query compilation, optimization, and maintains all metadata about databases, schemas, and micro-partitions.

**C** is correct because Cloud Services enforces authentication and evaluates role-based access control for every request.

**B** is incorrect because persisting table files in object storage is the responsibility of the Storage layer, not Cloud Services.

**D** is incorrect because query operators are executed by virtual warehouses (the Compute layer), not by Cloud Services.

[Cloud Services Layer](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts)

questions.stem: What does auto-resume on a warehouse do?

options.text: Drops cached query results after inactivity
Starts compute automatically when a query needs it
Replicates warehouse settings to other accounts
Converts a single-cluster warehouse into multi-cluster

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the `AUTO_RESUME` property automatically restarts a suspended warehouse when a SQL statement or query is submitted to it, so users do not need to manually resume it.

**A** is incorrect because dropping cached results is not related to auto-resume; result cache persistence is governed by separate mechanisms.

**C** is incorrect because auto-resume has no effect on replication — it only controls whether a suspended warehouse starts on demand.

**D** is incorrect because transitioning to multi-cluster mode requires changing the `MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT` parameter, not auto-resume.

[Working with Warehouses](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-tasks)

questions.stem: Which Snowflake object is best for organizing related tables and views within a database?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because a Schema is the logical namespace inside a database that groups related tables, views, stages, and other data objects together.

**A** is incorrect because a Warehouse provides compute resources; it is not part of the data object containment hierarchy.

**C** is incorrect because a Role is a security principal used to grant privileges, not a container for data objects.

**D** is incorrect because a Pipe is a Snowpipe object for continuous ingestion; it does not organize tables or views.

[Key Concepts & Architecture](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/intro-key-concepts)

questions.stem: Why can two teams run large queries at the same time without one warehouse blocking the other?

options.text: Because all queries run in Cloud Services
Because each team can use a separate virtual warehouse against shared storage
Because Snowflake duplicates the database for each user
Because every table has its own built-in warehouse

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Snowflake's separation of compute and storage allows multiple independent virtual warehouses to query the same data in Storage concurrently without resource contention.

**A** is incorrect because Cloud Services handles coordination and metadata, not direct query execution — warehouses do the actual compute work.

**C** is incorrect because Snowflake does not duplicate database objects per user; all warehouses read the same shared storage.

**D** is incorrect because tables do not have dedicated built-in warehouses; compute resources are provided by separately provisioned virtual warehouses.

[Multi-Cluster Warehouses](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-multicluster)

questions.stem: Which statement best describes micro-partitions?

options.text: Users must define them manually before loading data
They are automatically maintained units of columnar storage used for metadata and pruning
They are identical to traditional database partitions
They only exist for temporary tables

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because micro-partitions are contiguous units of storage (50–500 MB of uncompressed data) that Snowflake automatically creates and maintains, storing rich metadata used for partition pruning during queries.

**A** is incorrect because micro-partitions are created automatically by Snowflake as data is loaded — no manual definition is required.

**C** is incorrect because micro-partitions differ from traditional partitions: they are immutable, automatically managed, and use columnar storage with rich metadata rather than user-defined row-based ranges.

**D** is incorrect because micro-partitions exist for all table types including permanent, transient, and external tables.

[Micro-partitions & Data Clustering](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-micro-partitions)

questions.stem: Which client experience is Snowflake's web-based user interface?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Snowsight is Snowflake's browser-based web UI, providing worksheets, dashboards, data exploration, and administration capabilities.

**A** is incorrect because Snowpark is a developer API framework for Python, Java, and Scala — not a web UI.

**C** is incorrect because SnowCD is the Snowflake Connectivity Diagnostic tool used to test network connectivity, not a query interface.

**D** is incorrect because Snowpipe Streaming is an SDK-based API for low-latency continuous data ingestion, not a web interface.

[Introduction to Snowsight](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/ui-snowsight)

questions.stem: Which stage is scoped to a single table by default?
questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because a table stage is automatically created with every table (referenced as `@%<table_name>`) and is scoped exclusively to that table.

**A** is incorrect because a user stage is scoped to an individual user (`@~`) and can be used to load data into any table that user has access to.

**B** is incorrect because a named internal stage is a separately created object with its own name and can be shared across multiple tables and operations.

**D** is incorrect because an external stage points to a customer-managed cloud storage location such as S3, Azure Blob, or GCS and is not scoped to a single table.

[Data Loading: Local File System — Create a Stage](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-load-local-file-system-create-stage)

questions.stem: Which command is used for bulk loading files into a table?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because `COPY INTO <table>` is the primary command for bulk loading staged files into a Snowflake table.

**A** is incorrect because `MERGE INTO` performs upsert DML operations on existing table rows — it does not load staged files.

**C** is incorrect because `PUT` uploads files from a local machine to an internal stage, which is a staging step before loading — it does not load data into a table.

**D** is incorrect because `GET` downloads files from an internal stage to the local filesystem — it is the reverse of PUT and does not load tables.

[COPY INTO <table>](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/copy-into-table)

questions.stem: What is a primary benefit of Snowpipe compared with scheduled bulk COPY jobs?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Snowpipe is designed for continuous, near-real-time ingestion triggered by file arrival events (cloud storage notifications or REST API calls), unlike scheduled batch COPY jobs that run at fixed intervals.

**A** is incorrect because Snowpipe still requires a stage to be defined — the pipe object references the stage and a COPY INTO statement.

**C** is incorrect because Snowpipe supports all file formats that `COPY INTO` supports, including CSV, Parquet, JSON, Avro, and others.

**D** is incorrect because Snowpipe handles ingestion only; transformations still require virtual warehouses or Snowpark.

[Introduction to Snowpipe](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-load-snowpipe-intro)

questions.stem: Which file formats can Snowflake load natively without converting files to CSV first? (Select TWO)
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Snowflake natively supports loading Parquet files, including directly into a VARIANT column or with schema detection.

**B** is correct because JSON is a first-class semi-structured format that Snowflake can ingest natively into VARIANT columns.

**C** is incorrect because executable binaries are not a supported data format for loading into Snowflake tables.

**D** is incorrect because PowerPoint slides are presentation documents, not a supported data ingestion format in Snowflake.

[Semi-structured Data Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/semistructured-intro)

questions.stem: A team wants a table with no Fail-safe and lower recovery cost for transient staging data. Which table type fits best?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because transient tables have a maximum Time Travel retention of 1 day and no Fail-safe period, making them cost-effective for staging data that does not need extended recovery protection.

**A** is incorrect because permanent tables include Fail-safe (7 days for Enterprise and above) and support up to 90 days of Time Travel — both of which incur additional storage costs.

**C** is incorrect because external tables reference data in an external stage and do not support Time Travel or Fail-safe; they are used for querying data that lives outside Snowflake, not for staging.

**D** is incorrect because Hybrid Table is a separate table type optimized for transactional (HTAP) workloads, not for cost-saving on transient staging data.

[Fail-safe](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe)

questions.stem: Which COPY option controls behavior when a few records in a file are malformed?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the `ON_ERROR` option in `COPY INTO` specifies how Snowflake should handle errors — for example `CONTINUE` to skip bad rows, `SKIP_FILE` to bypass files with errors, or `ABORT_STATEMENT` (the default) to fail the entire load.

**B** is incorrect because `AUTO_RESUME` is a warehouse property that automatically restarts a suspended warehouse, not a COPY INTO error-handling option.

**C** is incorrect because `WAREHOUSE_SIZE` is a warehouse property for sizing compute clusters, not a COPY option related to data errors.

**D** is incorrect because `PURGE` controls whether successfully loaded files are deleted from the stage after loading — it does not govern error handling for malformed records.

[COPY INTO <table> — ON_ERROR](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/copy-into-table)

questions.stem: What does the PUT command do in Snowflake?

options.text: Uploads local files to an internal stage

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because `PUT` copies one or more files from the local machine (or a local path) to a Snowflake internal stage, compressing and encrypting them in transit.

**B** is incorrect because creating an external stage integration requires `CREATE STORAGE INTEGRATION` and `CREATE STAGE` DDL statements — not `PUT`.

**C** is incorrect because moving data between tables uses DML commands like `INSERT INTO ... SELECT` or `COPY INTO <table>` from another table — not `PUT`.

**D** is incorrect because Snowpipe notifications are triggered by cloud event notifications (such as S3 SQS) or REST API calls — the `PUT` command has no role in triggering pipes.

[PUT](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/put)

questions.stem: Which data type is commonly used to store semi-structured JSON in Snowflake?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the VARIANT data type can store any semi-structured value — JSON, Avro, ORC, XML, or Parquet — up to 16 MB per value, and supports dot/colon notation for querying nested elements.

**A** is incorrect because `OBJECT_ID` is not a Snowflake data type; there is no such built-in type for semi-structured storage.

**C** is incorrect because GEOGRAPHY is a specialized geospatial data type for storing and querying geographic locations, not JSON documents.

**D** is incorrect because VECTOR is a data type for storing embedding arrays used in similarity search, not for general semi-structured JSON content.

[Semi-structured Data Types](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/data-types-semistructured)

questions.stem: Why might a team choose an external stage instead of an internal stage?

options.text: To reference files in cloud storage they already manage
Because COPY INTO cannot read internal stages
Because external stages are required for temporary tables

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because an external stage points to a customer-managed cloud storage location such as Amazon S3, Azure Blob Storage, or Google Cloud Storage, allowing teams to load data directly from storage they already control without duplicating files.

**B** is incorrect because external stages typically require credentials or a storage integration to authenticate access to the cloud storage location — they do not eliminate credential management.

**C** is incorrect because `COPY INTO` works with both internal and external stages — internal stages are a fully supported source.

**D** is incorrect because temporary tables have no dependency on external stages; they can be loaded from internal or external stages.

[COPY INTO <table>](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/copy-into-table)

questions.stem: Which statement about temporary tables is correct?

options.text: They are visible to all users in an account
They persist until manually dropped across sessions
They exist only for the duration of the session that created them

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because temporary tables are session-scoped: they are automatically dropped when the session ends and are visible only to the session that created them.

**A** is incorrect because temporary tables are private to the creating session — other users cannot see or query them.

**B** is incorrect because temporary tables are automatically purged at session end; they cannot persist across sessions.

**D** is incorrect because temporary tables have no Fail-safe period, making them unsuitable for data that requires recovery protection.

[Temporary and Transient Tables](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-temp-transient)

questions.stem: What does setting PURGE = TRUE in a COPY INTO statement do?

options.text: Removes successfully loaded files from the stage after loading
Deletes existing rows from the target table before loading
Disables Time Travel for the target table
Skips files that were already loaded previously

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because `PURGE = TRUE` instructs Snowflake to delete files from the stage after they are successfully loaded, helping to keep the stage clean and avoid accumulating old files.

**B** is incorrect because PURGE only affects stage files — it does not truncate or delete rows from the target table before loading.

**C** is incorrect because Time Travel is controlled by the `DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS` parameter on the table, not by COPY INTO options.

**D** is incorrect because tracking previously loaded files is handled by Snowflake's load history metadata (used by the default `LOAD_UNCERTAIN_FILES` behavior), not by the PURGE option.

[COPY INTO <table>](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/copy-into-table)

questions.stem: Which clause filters grouped results after aggregation?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because `HAVING` filters groups produced by `GROUP BY` after aggregation is computed — for example, `HAVING COUNT(*) > 5` keeps only groups with more than five rows.

**A** is incorrect because `WHERE` filters individual rows before grouping and aggregation occurs, so aggregate functions cannot be used in a WHERE clause.

**C** is incorrect because `QUALIFY` filters the result of window functions (after they are computed), not aggregate groups.

**D** is incorrect because `LIMIT` restricts the number of rows returned in the final result set — it does not filter based on aggregate conditions.

[SQL Query Syntax](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/select)

questions.stem: Which join returns only rows with matching keys in both tables?
questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because an `INNER JOIN` returns only rows where the join condition is satisfied in both tables — unmatched rows from either side are excluded.

**A** is incorrect because a `LEFT OUTER JOIN` preserves all rows from the left table, even when there is no matching row in the right table (right-side columns are NULL for unmatched rows).

**B** is incorrect because a `FULL OUTER JOIN` returns all rows from both tables, filling NULLs for the side that has no match.

**D** is incorrect because a `CROSS JOIN` produces a Cartesian product of both tables with no join condition — every row from the left is paired with every row from the right.

[SQL Query Syntax — JOIN](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/select)

questions.stem: How do you access the `city` field inside a VARIANT column named `payload`?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Snowflake uses colon notation (`payload:city`) to traverse top-level keys in a VARIANT column. Bracket notation (`payload['city']`) is also valid.

**A** is incorrect because dot notation (`payload.city`) would be interpreted as a table-alias.column reference rather than a VARIANT path traversal.

**C** is incorrect because `city(payload)` is not valid SQL syntax for extracting a field from a semi-structured value.

**D** is incorrect because `->` is JSON extraction operator syntax from other databases (e.g., MySQL, PostgreSQL) and is not supported in Snowflake SQL.

[Querying Semi-structured Data](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/querying-semistructured)

questions.stem: Which functions are aggregate functions in SQL? (Select TWO)
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because `COUNT` is an aggregate function that counts the number of rows (or non-NULL values) across a group.

**B** is correct because `SUM` is an aggregate function that adds up numeric values across all rows in a group.

**C** is incorrect because `COALESCE` is a scalar function that returns the first non-NULL value from a list of expressions — it operates on individual rows, not across groups.

**D** is incorrect because `SUBSTR` (or `SUBSTRING`) is a scalar string function that extracts a portion of a string value — it is not an aggregate function.

[Aggregate Functions](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/functions-aggregation)

questions.stem: Which clause is most appropriate for sorting query output from highest revenue to lowest revenue?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because `ORDER BY revenue DESC` sorts the final result set by the revenue column in descending order (largest value first).

**A** is incorrect because `GROUP BY` defines grouping keys for aggregation and does not sort output — `DESC` is not valid syntax as part of a GROUP BY clause.

**C** is incorrect because `HAVING` filters groups based on aggregate conditions; it is not used for sorting and does not accept sort direction.

**D** is incorrect because `QUALIFY` filters rows based on window function results and does not sort the result set.

[SQL Query Syntax — ORDER BY](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/select)

questions.stem: Which result is produced by a LEFT JOIN when a left-side row has no match on the right?

options.text: The row is removed from the result set
The row is returned with NULLs for right-side columns
The query fails with an error
The row is duplicated once per warehouse cluster

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because a `LEFT JOIN` (LEFT OUTER JOIN) preserves every row from the left table; for rows with no matching row on the right, all right-side column values appear as NULL in the result.

**A** is incorrect because removing unmatched rows is the behavior of an INNER JOIN, not a LEFT JOIN.

**C** is incorrect because having no match on the right side is a normal, expected outcome for a LEFT JOIN — it does not cause an error.

**D** is incorrect because row duplication related to warehouse clusters is not a concept in Snowflake SQL behavior.

[SQL Query Syntax — JOIN](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/select)

questions.stem: Which expression is best for returning only the date portion of a timestamp column?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because `TO_DATE` converts or casts a timestamp value to a DATE, stripping the time component and returning only the year-month-day portion.

**B** is incorrect because `OBJECT_CONSTRUCT` builds a VARIANT object from key-value pairs — it has no date extraction purpose.

**C** is incorrect because `PARSE_JSON` converts a JSON string into a VARIANT value — it cannot be applied to a timestamp to extract a date.

**D** is incorrect because `BASE64_ENCODE` encodes a binary or string value to Base64 format — it is unrelated to date extraction from timestamps.

[Date & Time Functions](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/functions-date-time)

questions.stem: A query groups sales data by region and calculates AVG(sales). Which clause determines the grouping key?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because `GROUP BY region` partitions all rows into groups by their region value, and the aggregate `AVG(sales)` is then computed independently for each group.

**B** is incorrect because `ORDER BY region` sorts the output rows — it does not create groups for aggregation.

**C** is incorrect because `LIMIT` restricts the number of rows returned — `LIMIT region` is invalid SQL syntax and has nothing to do with grouping.

**D** is incorrect because `FROM region` would reference a table named `region`, not define a grouping key.

[SQL Query Syntax — GROUP BY](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/select)

questions.stem: Which function converts a string containing JSON into a semi-structured VARIANT value?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because `PARSE_JSON` takes a JSON-formatted string and returns a VARIANT value, enabling colon-path querying and semi-structured operations.

**B** is incorrect because `TO_GEOGRAPHY` converts a WKT, WKB, or GeoJSON string into a Snowflake GEOGRAPHY value — not a general VARIANT.

**C** is incorrect because `FLATTEN` is a table function that laterally explodes arrays or objects inside a VARIANT into multiple rows; it does not parse a raw JSON string.

**D** is incorrect because `SYSTEM$WAIT` is a system function that pauses execution for a specified number of seconds — it has no JSON parsing capability.

[PARSE_JSON](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/functions/parse_json)

questions.stem: What does FLATTEN help do with semi-structured data?

options.text: Expand arrays or nested objects into individual rows
Automatically resize warehouses for JSON queries
Convert SQL queries into Python code

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because `FLATTEN` is a table function that laterally joins a VARIANT column's array or object elements as individual rows, making it possible to query nested data in a relational style.

**A** is incorrect because encryption at rest is handled automatically by Snowflake for all data; FLATTEN has no encryption role.

**C** is incorrect because warehouse resizing is controlled by the `ALTER WAREHOUSE` command or auto-scaling settings, not by FLATTEN.

**D** is incorrect because FLATTEN is a SQL table function — it does not translate SQL to Python.

[FLATTEN](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/functions/flatten)

questions.stem: Which clause limits a result set to the first 10 rows returned?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because `LIMIT 10` is standard SQL syntax (also supported as `FETCH FIRST 10 ROWS ONLY`) that restricts the number of rows in the result set to 10.

**A** is incorrect because `TOP 10 ONLY` is not valid Snowflake SQL syntax; Snowflake does not support the `SELECT TOP N` form.

**C** is incorrect because `ROWS 10` is not a valid Snowflake SQL clause for row limiting.

**D** is incorrect because `HAVING COUNT(*) <= 10` filters aggregate groups where the count is 10 or fewer — it does not limit the number of rows in the output.

[SQL Query Syntax — LIMIT](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/select)

questions.stem: When converting a timestamp between time zones, which Snowflake data concern is most relevant?

options.text: Timestamp type and timezone handling functions

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Snowflake has three timestamp types — `TIMESTAMP_NTZ` (no timezone), `TIMESTAMP_LTZ` (local timezone), and `TIMESTAMP_TZ` (with offset stored) — and functions like `CONVERT_TIMEZONE` handle conversion between zones correctly depending on which type is used.

**A** is incorrect because warehouse billing is based on compute time, not on timezone conversions within SQL.

**C** is incorrect because Marketplace listing regions are a distribution concern, unrelated to timestamp type handling in queries.

**D** is incorrect because micro-partition file size relates to storage optimization and query pruning, not to timezone conversion logic.

[Date & Time Data Types](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/data-types-datetime)

questions.stem: Which Snowflake security model is used to grant privileges to users through roles?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Snowflake uses Role-Based Access Control (RBAC): privileges are granted to roles, and roles are then granted to users, so users inherit the access rights of their active role.

**A** is incorrect because while Snowflake supports discretionary access control (DAC) alongside RBAC, it does not use Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) as its primary model.

**C** is incorrect because MAC address filtering is a network-layer concept; Snowflake's security model operates at the role and privilege level, not at the network hardware level.

**D** is incorrect because "row hash security" is not a Snowflake concept; row-level security in Snowflake is implemented via row access policies, which are a distinct feature.

[Access Control Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: Which system role is primarily responsible for managing grants, users, and roles in Snowflake?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because SECURITYADMIN has the ability to manage all users, roles, and grants across the account — it is the primary role for security administration.

**A** is incorrect because PUBLIC is a default role automatically granted to all users with minimal privileges; it is not an administrative role.

**C** is incorrect because SYSADMIN focuses on creating and managing databases, schemas, and warehouses — it handles object administration rather than security grants.

**D** is incorrect because USERADMIN can manage users and roles but has a narrower scope than SECURITYADMIN, which can also manage grants on all objects.

[Access Control Overview — System-Defined Roles](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: What does MFA (multi-factor authentication) primarily add to username/password authentication?

options.text: A second verification factor beyond the password

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because MFA adds a second factor — typically a time-based one-time passcode (TOTP) from an authenticator app like Duo — that must be verified in addition to the password, significantly reducing the risk of credential-only attacks.

**B** is incorrect because automatic warehouse scaling is controlled by multi-cluster warehouse settings (`MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT`), which are entirely unrelated to MFA.

**C** is incorrect because Snowflake encrypts all data in transit using TLS by default for all editions, independent of whether MFA is enabled.

**D** is incorrect because secure data sharing is a separate Snowflake capability that has no connection to authentication methods like MFA.

[Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-mfa)

questions.stem: Which statement about network policies is correct?

options.text: They control SQL syntax allowed in worksheets
They can restrict which client IP addresses are allowed to connect
They apply only to external stage connections

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Snowflake network policies define allowed (and optionally blocked) IP address ranges, restricting which clients can connect to an account or specific users.

**A** is incorrect because network policies operate at the network connection level — they do not control SQL syntax or worksheet behavior.

**C** is incorrect because network policies are a network access control mechanism and work alongside RBAC, not as a replacement for role-based grants.

**D** is incorrect because network policies apply to all Snowflake connections (Snowsight, SnowSQL, JDBC, etc.) — they are not limited to external stage operations.

[Network Policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

questions.stem: How is data encrypted when moving between a client and Snowflake?

options.text: It is not encrypted unless Business Critical is enabled

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Snowflake enforces TLS (Transport Layer Security) encryption for all data in transit between clients and the Snowflake service, on all editions by default.

**B** is incorrect because customer-managed keys (Tri-Secret Secure) address encryption at rest and key custody — not general in-transit encryption, which is TLS-based and always active.

**C** is incorrect because TLS encryption in transit is enforced on all Snowflake editions, not only Business Critical.

**D** is incorrect because local disk cache refers to client-side storage, which is unrelated to transit encryption between the client and the Snowflake service.

[Security in Snowflake](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-encryption)

questions.stem: Which features provide fine-grained protection of sensitive data values within Snowflake? (Select TWO)
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because dynamic data masking applies masking policies to columns, obfuscating or replacing sensitive values (such as PII) for users without the appropriate role.

**B** is correct because row access policies filter which rows a user can see in a query result, enabling row-level security based on the querying user's role or attributes.

**C** is incorrect because warehouse auto-suspend is a cost control feature that suspends idle compute — it has no data protection or access control function.

**D** is incorrect because resource monitors track and limit credit consumption — they are a cost governance tool, not a data security feature.

[Column-level Security](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-column-intro)

questions.stem: Why is it usually better to grant privileges to a custom role instead of directly to many individual users?

options.text: It increases storage compression for those users
It simplifies administration and supports least-privilege design
It is required in order to load JSON data
It disables inherited privileges from parent roles

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because granting privileges to a role and then granting that role to users allows administrators to manage access in one place; adding or removing a user from the role instantly adjusts their access, supporting least-privilege and reducing administrative overhead.

**A** is incorrect because storage compression is a physical storage optimization independent of how privileges are structured.

**C** is incorrect because loading JSON data is controlled by stage and table privileges — no requirement exists to use custom roles specifically for JSON loading.

**D** is incorrect because granting a role to a user causes the user to inherit all privileges of that role; it does not disable privilege inheritance.

[Access Control Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: Which authentication method is commonly used for service accounts and automation scripts that should avoid storing passwords?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because key-pair authentication uses an RSA public/private key pair — the private key is stored securely on the client and never transmitted, making it well-suited for service accounts and CI/CD pipelines that need to avoid password storage.

**B** is incorrect because "temporary table tokens" is not a Snowflake authentication mechanism.

**C** is incorrect because the Search Optimization Service is a query performance feature for point-lookup queries — it has no role in authentication.

**D** is incorrect because "data exchange credentials" is not a Snowflake authentication mechanism; Data Exchange is a sharing platform.

[Key-pair Authentication](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/key-pair-auth)

questions.stem: What is the PUBLIC role in Snowflake?

options.text: A built-in role automatically granted to all users
The highest administrative role in the account
A network policy object for IP filtering
A special type of virtual warehouse

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because PUBLIC is a system-defined role that is automatically granted to every user in a Snowflake account, so any privileges granted to PUBLIC are accessible by all users — administrators should be cautious when granting anything to this role.

**B** is incorrect because ACCOUNTADMIN is the highest administrative role; PUBLIC has essentially no elevated privileges by default.

**C** is incorrect because network policies are separate security objects that control IP-based access; they are not roles.

**D** is incorrect because PUBLIC is a security role, not a warehouse type.

[Access Control Overview — System-Defined Roles](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: Which Snowflake capability lets a provider share live data without copying table files into the consumer account?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Snowflake's secure data sharing allows a provider to expose live database objects (tables, views, secure views) to consumer accounts by reference — no physical data is copied or moved.

**B** is incorrect because Fail-safe is a storage durability feature that allows Snowflake to recover data after extreme events; it is not related to sharing data with other accounts.

**C** is incorrect because warehouse replication copies warehouse configuration between accounts for disaster recovery or business continuity purposes, not for sharing data access.

**D** is incorrect because PUT and GET are file transfer commands for moving files to and from internal stages — they are not a sharing mechanism.

[Introduction to Secure Data Sharing](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-sharing-intro)

questions.stem: When is a reader account most useful?

options.text: When the consumer already has its own Snowflake account
When a consumer needs shared data but does not have a Snowflake account
When publishing notebooks for internal teams
When creating a transient staging table

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because a reader account is a managed account created by a provider specifically for consumers who do not have their own Snowflake account, enabling them to query shared data using compute paid for by the provider.

**A** is incorrect because if the consumer already has a Snowflake account, the provider can share directly with that account using standard secure data sharing — no reader account is needed.

**C** is incorrect because publishing notebooks for internal teams uses Snowsight or similar internal tooling, not reader accounts.

**D** is incorrect because transient tables are a local table type in the provider's account; they are unrelated to reader account use cases.

[Creating and Managing Reader Accounts](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-sharing-reader-create)

questions.stem: What is the Snowflake Marketplace used for?

options.text: Purchasing warehouse compute credits from other customers
Discovering and consuming third-party data products and applications

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Snowflake Marketplace is a catalog where data providers publish listings — including datasets, data applications, and native apps — that consumers can discover and access directly in their Snowflake account.

**A** is incorrect because compute credits are billed directly through Snowflake pricing agreements; there is no peer-to-peer credit marketplace.

**C** is incorrect because external stages are configured via DDL (`CREATE STAGE`) and storage integrations — the Marketplace has no role in this.

**D** is incorrect because encryption key rotation is managed through Snowflake's key management or Tri-Secret Secure, not through the Marketplace.

[Snowflake Marketplace](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-marketplace)

questions.stem: Which object is created by a provider to expose databases, schemas, and secure views to consumers?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a Share is a named Snowflake object (`CREATE SHARE`) into which the provider grants access to databases, schemas, tables, and secure views; the share is then granted to consumer accounts.

**B** is incorrect because a Pipe is a Snowpipe object for automated data ingestion — it is not a data sharing mechanism.

**C** is incorrect because a masking policy is a column-level security feature that obscures data values — it is a complement to sharing but is not itself the sharing object.

**D** is incorrect because a task graph is a directed acyclic graph of Tasks used for workflow orchestration — it has no role in data sharing.

[Introduction to Secure Data Sharing](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-sharing-intro)

questions.stem: What is one key difference between a Data Exchange and the Snowflake Marketplace?

options.text: Data Exchange is for private collaboration groups, while Marketplace is a broader public discovery channel
Marketplace requires no Snowflake account to consume listings
Data Exchange can only share CSV files
Marketplace listings cannot include native applications

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a Data Exchange is a private, invitation-only hub for a defined community of providers and consumers, while the Snowflake Marketplace is an open discovery platform accessible to all Snowflake customers.

**B** is incorrect because both Marketplace and Data Exchange require a Snowflake account to consume shared data.

**C** is incorrect because Data Exchange shares live database objects (tables, secure views) — not raw files in a specific format like CSV.

**D** is incorrect because native applications built with the Native App Framework can be distributed through the Snowflake Marketplace.

[Snowflake Marketplace](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-marketplace)

questions.stem: Which objects are commonly shared instead of raw base tables to enforce governed access in a share? (Select TWO)
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because secure views allow providers to expose only specific columns or filtered rows without revealing the underlying table structure or implementation, enforcing governance over what consumers can see.

**D** is correct because secure UDFs (user-defined functions) can be shared as callable functions in a share, enabling providers to expose computation logic while hiding implementation details.

**B** is incorrect because virtual warehouses provide compute resources and cannot be shared as part of a data share object — consumers use their own compute.

**C** is incorrect because resource monitors are cost-control objects in an account and are not shareable data objects.

[Introduction to Secure Data Sharing](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-sharing-intro)

questions.stem: A provider wants visibility into how often their Marketplace listing is being used by consumers. Which Snowflake feature is most relevant?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the Snowflake Marketplace provides provider analytics dashboards that show listing installs, consumer query activity, and usage trends, helping providers understand adoption.

**B** is incorrect because micro-partition pruning statistics relate to query performance optimization, not Marketplace listing usage.

**C** is incorrect because Time Travel provides access to historical data states within an account — it does not report on Marketplace listing consumption.

**D** is incorrect because warehouse auto-resume history tracks when suspended warehouses were restarted — it is unrelated to Marketplace analytics.

[Snowflake Marketplace](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-marketplace)

questions.stem: Which term best describes granting another account access to selected objects while the provider retains ownership?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because secure data sharing grants a consumer account read access to the provider's database objects by reference — the provider retains full ownership and no data is duplicated.

**A** is incorrect because database replication creates a physical copy of a database in another account or region for disaster recovery or business continuity — it transfers a copy, not just access.

**C** is incorrect because data unloading (using `COPY INTO <location>`) exports data to files in a stage or cloud storage — the consumer receives files, not live access to the source objects.

**D** is incorrect because "session cloning" is not a Snowflake concept.

[Introduction to Secure Data Sharing](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-sharing-intro)

questions.stem: Why are secure views often preferred over base tables in shared datasets?

options.text: They let providers hide underlying table logic and restrict exposed columns or rows
They automatically resize the provider's warehouse
They eliminate the need for any privilege grants
They physically copy source rows into the consumer account

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because secure views prevent consumers from inspecting the view definition (unlike standard views), allowing providers to expose only specific columns or filtered rows while concealing the underlying table structure and business logic.

**B** is incorrect because warehouse sizing is an independent configuration concern — secure views have no effect on compute resource allocation.

**C** is incorrect because secure views still require proper privilege grants; the `SECURE` keyword protects the view definition, not the need for access control.

**D** is incorrect because secure data sharing never copies data — consumers query the provider's live storage by reference.

[Working with Secure Views](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/views-secure)

questions.stem: What is Snowpark primarily used for?

options.text: Building DataFrame-style applications in Python, Java, or Scala that run inside Snowflake
Uploading local files to internal stages
Replacing all SQL queries in Snowsight worksheets

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Snowpark is Snowflake's developer framework that allows data engineers and scientists to write DataFrame-based and procedural logic in Python, Java, or Scala that is pushed down and executed inside Snowflake.

**B** is incorrect because uploading local files to internal stages is done with the `PUT` command via SnowSQL or other clients — not Snowpark.

**C** is incorrect because Snowpark is an API for programmatic application development; Snowsight worksheets remain the interactive SQL interface and are not replaced by Snowpark.

**D** is incorrect because MFA enrollment is managed through user account settings or SCIM provisioning — not through Snowpark.

[Snowpark Developer Guide](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowpark/python/index)

questions.stem: Which Snowflake capability lets developers build and host Python-based interactive web apps directly in the platform?
questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Streamlit in Snowflake allows developers to build, host, and share interactive Python-based web applications natively within their Snowflake account, with direct access to Snowflake data.

**A** is incorrect because Snowpipe Streaming is an SDK-based API for low-latency row-by-row data ingestion — it is an ingestion service, not an app hosting platform.

**C** is incorrect because Tri-Secret Secure is a Business Critical feature for customer-managed encryption key custody — it is unrelated to app development.

**D** is incorrect because failover groups are used for business continuity and disaster recovery replication between accounts — not for hosting applications.

[Streamlit in Snowflake](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/streamlit/about-streamlit)

questions.stem: What is the Snowflake Native App Framework designed for?

options.text: Creating and distributing applications that run in consumer Snowflake accounts
Scheduling automatic warehouse suspension at set times
Encrypting external stages with custom TLS certificates
Replacing role hierarchy with application-defined roles only

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the Native App Framework allows developers to build, package, and distribute Snowflake applications — including logic, data, and UIs — that consumers can install directly into their own Snowflake accounts from the Marketplace or private listings.

**B** is incorrect because automatic warehouse suspension is controlled by the `AUTO_SUSPEND` property and resource monitors, not by the Native App Framework.

**C** is incorrect because external stage encryption uses storage integrations and cloud provider IAM mechanisms — the Native App Framework has no role in TLS certificate management for stages.

**D** is incorrect because the Native App Framework uses application roles for privilege management within an app, but it does not replace the account-level role hierarchy.

[Native App Framework](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/native-apps-about)

questions.stem: Which tool is most directly associated with command-line automation of Snowflake resources and native app packaging?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Snowflake CLI (`snow`) is the modern command-line tool for managing Snowflake objects, running SQL, and packaging and deploying Snowpark functions, Streamlit apps, and Native App Framework applications.

**B** is incorrect because "Marketplace Studio" is not a distinct Snowflake CLI tool; listing management is done via Snowsight's Provider Studio UI or the Snowflake CLI.

**C** is incorrect because reader accounts are a sharing concept for providing non-Snowflake customers access to shared data — they are not an automation or packaging tool.

**D** is incorrect because the query result cache is a performance feature that automatically stores query results for reuse — it is not a CLI or automation tool.

[Snowflake CLI](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowflake-cli/index)

questions.stem: Which tools are examples of partner or adjacent technologies commonly integrated with Snowflake for data transformation and visualization? (Select TWO)
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because dbt (data build tool) is a widely used open-source SQL transformation framework that integrates directly with Snowflake as a partner tool for building data models in the warehouse.

**B** is correct because Tableau is a leading BI and data visualization tool that connects to Snowflake through a native connector and is listed as a Technology Partner.

**C** is incorrect because micro-partitions are an internal Snowflake storage concept, not a partner or external technology integration.

**D** is incorrect because Snowsight worksheets are a native Snowflake UI feature — not a partner or adjacent external tool.

[Snowflake Technology Partners](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/ecosystem-etl)

questions.stem: Why might a team choose Streamlit in Snowflake instead of hosting a separate dashboard server externally?

options.text: To keep the app co-located with Snowflake data and eliminate a separate hosting infrastructure
Because Streamlit in Snowflake completely replaces SQL queries
Because it requires no Python code to build apps
Because it only works for external data consumers outside the account

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because hosting the Streamlit app inside Snowflake eliminates the need for a separate server or cloud hosting service, keeps the application close to governed data, and simplifies deployment and access management.

**B** is incorrect because Streamlit in Snowflake apps still use SQL (and Python) to query Snowflake data — Streamlit does not replace SQL.

**C** is incorrect because Streamlit apps require Python code; Streamlit is a Python library for building interactive web UIs.

**D** is incorrect because Streamlit in Snowflake apps are primarily designed for internal users within the Snowflake account, though they can be shared selectively.

[Streamlit in Snowflake](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/streamlit/about-streamlit)

questions.stem: Which statement best distinguishes Snowpark from standard SQL worksheets in Snowsight?

options.text: Snowpark is for programmatic DataFrame and procedural development, while worksheets provide an interactive SQL environment
Snowpark is a security feature for encrypting worksheets
Worksheets are required in order to load staged data files into tables
Snowpark cannot call any built-in SQL functions

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Snowpark is a developer API that allows data engineers and scientists to write programmatic DataFrame logic and stored procedures in Python, Java, or Scala; Snowsight worksheets provide an interactive browser-based environment for writing and running SQL.

**B** is incorrect because Snowpark is not a security feature — it is a developer framework for writing code that runs in Snowflake.

**C** is incorrect because staged data can be loaded using `COPY INTO` from any Snowflake client (SnowSQL, JDBC, Snowsight, or Snowpark) — worksheets are not required.

**D** is incorrect because Snowpark can call all built-in SQL functions via the `functions` module and supports SQL expression interoperability.

[Snowpark Developer Guide](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowpark/python/index)

questions.stem: Which TWO properties of Snowpipe distinguish it from a scheduled COPY INTO batch job? (Select TWO)

options.text: It triggers automatically on file arrival via event notifications or REST API
It is billed using serverless compute credits rather than warehouse credits
It requires a running virtual warehouse to process each file
It only supports loading into external tables

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Snowpipe responds to cloud storage event notifications (e.g., S3 SQS/SNS, Azure Event Grid) or REST API calls to process new files as they arrive, enabling near-real-time continuous ingestion.

**B** is correct because Snowpipe uses Snowflake-managed serverless compute (billed by data volume and processing time at serverless rates) rather than requiring the customer to provision a running virtual warehouse.

**C** is incorrect because one of Snowpipe's key advantages is that it does not require a customer-managed virtual warehouse — it uses serverless compute provisioned by Snowflake.

**D** is incorrect because Snowpipe loads data into regular Snowflake tables, not external tables; external tables reference data in external storage and are not loaded by Snowpipe.

[Introduction to Snowpipe](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-load-snowpipe-intro)

questions.stem: Which TWO clauses can filter rows in a SELECT statement — one before aggregation and one after? (Select TWO)
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because `WHERE` filters individual rows before grouping and aggregation — it cannot reference aggregate functions.

**B** is correct because `HAVING` filters groups after aggregation is computed, and it can reference aggregate expressions like `HAVING COUNT(*) > 5`.

**C** is incorrect because `LIMIT` restricts the number of rows in the final output — it does not filter rows based on data conditions either before or after aggregation.

**D** is incorrect because `ORDER BY` sorts the result set — it does not filter rows at any stage of query execution.

[SQL Query Syntax](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/select)

questions.stem: Which TWO system-defined roles in Snowflake are focused on security and privilege management rather than object creation? (Select TWO)
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because SECURITYADMIN is designed to manage users, roles, and grants across the account — its primary purpose is security administration, not creating databases or warehouses.

**B** is correct because USERADMIN is specifically designed to create and manage users and roles — it focuses on identity management rather than data object administration.

**C** is incorrect because SYSADMIN is the system role focused on creating and managing databases, schemas, warehouses, and other data objects — it is an object administration role.

**D** is incorrect because PUBLIC is a default role automatically assigned to all users with essentially no elevated privileges — it is not a security administration role.

[Access Control Overview — System-Defined Roles](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview)

questions.stem: Which TWO features help reduce virtual warehouse compute costs when workloads are not running? (Select TWO)
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the `AUTO_SUSPEND` property automatically suspends a warehouse after a configured period of inactivity, stopping credit consumption when no queries are running.

**B** is correct because resource monitors allow administrators to set credit quotas and trigger actions (including suspending warehouses) when thresholds are reached, directly controlling compute spend.

**C** is incorrect because secure data sharing is a data access and distribution feature — it does not affect warehouse compute costs.

**D** is incorrect because micro-partition pruning is a query performance optimization that reduces the data scanned — it improves query efficiency but does not control warehouse suspension or credit consumption directly.

[Working with Warehouses](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-tasks)

questions.stem: Which TWO statements accurately describe how secure data sharing works in Snowflake? (Select TWO)

options.text: The consumer queries live provider data without any physical copy being made
Consumer accounts use their own compute to query shared data
Sharing always requires the consumer to pay the provider for storage costs
Shared data is exported as CSV files to the consumer account

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Snowflake's secure data sharing works by reference — the consumer account gets read access to the provider's live data objects without any ETL or physical data movement.

**B** is correct because the consumer's virtual warehouse (or reader account compute) executes queries against the provider's shared data, so the consumer's compute costs apply rather than the provider's.

**C** is incorrect because there is no mandatory provider-to-consumer storage billing in standard secure data sharing — the provider pays for their storage and the consumer pays for their own query compute.

**D** is incorrect because secure data sharing never exports or sends CSV files; consumers query the provider's live data directly via Snowflake's storage layer.

[Introduction to Secure Data Sharing](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-sharing-intro)
